10.1016/j.jmp.2017.12.001
The modelization of an economic agent’s preference structure is typically done by constructing a binary relation on the set of alternatives. A traditional tenet of rationality is that this relation satisfies the property of transitivity. If the property of completeness holds as well, then the agent’s preference structure has a continuous utility representation, provided that suitable hypotheses of separability are satisfied  (Beardon, Candeal, Herden, Induráin, & Mehta, 2002; Bridges & Mehta, 1995; Debreu, 1954) .

The advantage of having a utility representation carries a high cost. Indeed, the property of completeness – which is necessary to obtain a utility function – is far from being an overall accepted assumption. As a matter of fact, completeness started being questioned in the 40sby  von Neumann & Morgenstern (1944) , and lately excerpted from being a basic axiom of rationality (see, e.g., the seminal paper of  Aumann (1962) , as well as the more recent contribution of  Ok (2002) ).

On the other hand, the axiom of transitivity was possibly harder to abandon (even if it was questioned before completeness). In 1914, Wiener (and Poincaré before him, as discussed in  Pirlot and Vincke (1997) ) had already started dealing with cases of intransitive indifference  (Fishburn & Monjardet, 1992; Wiener, 1914) . However, it was only in the 50s that some authors explicitly began studying the axiomatics of intransitive forms of preferences. This approach is motivated by psychological experiments, which show that economic agents usually display intransitive forms of indifference (which is the symmetric part of the preference relation): see, among the many contributions,  Fishburn (1970a) ,  Krantz (1967) ,  Shafer (1974)  and  Tversky (1969) .

In this direction,  Luce (1956)  introduced in 1956 the notion of a  semiorder  in the realm of preference theory. His purpose was to describe scenarios in which the asymmetric part of a preference is transitive, but its symmetric part may fail to be so. In Luce’s model, indifference is regulated by the principle of the “just noticeable difference”: two alternatives are equally preferred in case the difference of their evaluations is below a suitable threshold of perception/discrimination. Later on,  Fishburn (1970b)  extended the notion of a semiorder by that of an  interval order . In the latter setting, each alternative is assigned an “interval of evaluations”, and all intervals are naturally ordered. The substantial difference between models based on semiorders and interval orders is that the threshold of discrimination need not be constant in the latter case.

Technically, an interval order is a Ferrers

                      interval order. Semiorders and interval orders have been extensively studied in the literature on preference theory, in relation to the existence of continuous/semicontinuous utility representations, as well as the possibility of applying these types of preference structures to a vast range of decision problems: see, among the several contributions to the topic, the monographs  (Fishburn, 1985; Pirlot & Vincke, 1997) , the treatise  (Aleskerov, Bouyssou, & Monjardet, 2007) , and the papers  (Beja & Gilboa, 1992; Bosi, Candeal, Induráin, Olóriz, & Zudaire, 2001; Candeal & Induráin, 2010; Fishburn, 1968, 1973, 1997; Gensemer, 1987; Giarlotta & Watson, 2016; Scott & Suppes, 1958) .

Recently,  Öztürk (2008) , in the process of dealing with some generalizations of the notion of semiorder proposed by  Fishburn (1997) , extended the classical Ferrers condition by the so-called  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      
                      -Ferrers properties . These properties consider the relationship between the first and the last element of two chains of preferences having length  
                         m 
                       and  
                         n 
                      , respectively. In particular, the Ferrers condition corresponds to  
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, whereas semitransitivity is equivalent to  
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers. However, Öztürk’s definition is limited to an asymmetric (and transitive) preference, and so it does not allow one to deal with “degrees of transitivity” of the associated indifference.

This motivated a further extension of her approach, undertaken in  Giarlotta and Watson (2014) . In that paper, the authors distinguish two types of  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties:  weak  and  strict . Weak properties are related to chains of reflexive preferences, whereas strict ones are related to chains of asymmetric preferences. While the two versions of  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties coincide for  
                         m 
                         + 
                         n 
                         = 
                         4 
                      , they behave quite differently as  
                         m 
                       and  
                         n 
                       increase.

Weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties form a finite lattice under implication, and this lattice has been fully described in  Giarlotta and Watson (2014) . These properties have also been studied in relation to other topics: stronger forms of interval orders and semiorders  (Giarlotta, 2014) ; special bi-preference structures called  NaP-preferences  ( necessary and possible preferences   (Giarlotta, 2014, 2015; Giarlotta & Greco, 2013; Giarlotta & Watson, 2017a, b) ); an axiomatic approach to individual choice  (Cantone, Giarlotta, Greco, & Watson, 2016)  developed within the framework of revealed preference theory  (Samuelson, 1938) .

In this paper, we carefully analyze strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties, which codify degrees of transitivity of asymmetric preferences. First of all, we show that any strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property implies acyclicity, but not necessarily transitivity (with two exceptions, for  
                         m 
                         = 
                         2 
                         , 
                         3 
                      ). However, if the transitivity of an asymmetric preference is assumed, then the situation is quite easy: in this case, strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties are decreasing in strength as  
                         m 
                       increases.

On the other hand, if the transitivity of an asymmetric preference is not assumed, then the situation becomes much more complex; in particular, strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties cease to decrease in strength as  
                         m 
                       increases. The strongest property of all is the strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, which is equivalent to the transitivity of both the strict preference and the associated indifference: in fact, this property implies all the other strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties. The strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property is the second strongest of all: in fact, it implies the transitivity of the strict preference, as well as all strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties for  
                         m 
                       larger than  
                         3 
                      . Starting for the strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            4 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property, monotonicity does not hold any longer (e.g., the latter property does not imply the strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            5 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property).

We show that strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties, despite their apparently erratic behavior, do display a form of regularity. In fact, they form a poset, which we fully describe in the main result of this paper ( Theorem 6.1 ). In particular, we solve positively the conjecture stated in  Giarlotta and Watson (2014) , which says that any integer  
                         m 
                         ≥ 
                         2 
                       can be assigned an integer  
                         
                            st 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            )

                            p 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers for  
                         p 
                         ≥ 
                         
                            st 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                      .

The semantics of (strict and weak) Ferrers properties is naturally related to economics and psychology. For instance, these properties are connected to the “money-pump phenomena”  (Cubit & Sugden, 2001; Davidson, McKinsey, & Suppes, 1955) , which have been extensively studied in economic theory and philosophy. 
                         3 
                       
                      
                         3 
                         See the article on preferences  (Hansson and Grüne-Yanoff, 2011)  in the  Standard Encyclopedia of Philosophy , where there is an extensive discussion of intransitive preferences and their relation to the money pump argument. 
                      In this paper, we give (see Section  3 ) the semantics of strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties in terms of transactions of goods. (See also  Cantone et al. (2016) , Section 4.3, for weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties.)

The paper is organized as follows. In Section  2  we recall some basic notions, and compare strict and weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties to each other. Section  3  describes an economic interpretation of strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties connected to money-pumps. In Section  4  we prove a few preliminary facts about strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties, and arrange them into a poset under implication. In Section  5  we compute the stabilizer of even integers. In Section  6  we state (without proof) the main result of this paper, which identifies all implications between strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties; as a corollary, we determine a formula to compute the stabilizer of all integers. Sections  7–9  are devoted to the proof of the main theorem: sufficiency is proved in Section  7 , whereas necessity is proved in Section  9 , using the results of Section  8 . Section  10  summarizes the findings of the paper and suggests further directions of research.

A  weak preference  is a reflexive binary relation on  
                         X 
                      , denoted by  
                         ≿ 
                      . We read  
                         x 
                         ≿ 
                         y 
                       as “alternative  
                         x 
                       is weakly preferred to alternative  
                         y 
                      ”, which means that  
                         x 
                       is at least as good as  
                         y 
                      . A  strict preference  is an asymmetric binary relation on  
                         X 
                      , denoted by  
                         ≻ 
                      . Then  
                         x 
                         ≻ 
                         y 
                       stands for “ 
                         x 
                       is strictly preferred to  
                         y 
                      ”, which means that  
                         x 
                       is certainly better than  
                         y 
                      .

                         ≿

                         A weak preference

                                     on

                               X

                                  a  (strict) linear order  if it is a complete strict partial order.

                                  a  (strict) interval order  if it is Ferrers; 
                               
                               
                                  – 
                                  a  (strict) semiorder  if it is Ferrers and semitransitive; 
                               
                               
                                  – 
                                  a  (strict) weak order  if it is asymmetric and negatively transitive (hence transitive); 
                               
                               
                                  – 
                                  a  (strict) linear order  if it is a complete strict partial order.

a  (strict) suborder  if it is acyclic;

a  (strict) partial order  if it is transitive;

a  (strict) interval order  if it is Ferrers;

a  (strict) semiorder  if it is Ferrers and semitransitive;

a  (strict) weak order  if it is asymmetric and negatively transitive (hence transitive);

a  (strict) linear order  if it is a complete strict partial order.

In what follows, we omit “weak” and “strict” whenever clear from context.

The next definition introduces the two versions of Ferrers properties, according to  Giarlotta and Watson (2014) . For the sake of synthesis, we write sequences of binary relationships in a compact way, e.g.,  
                         
                         x 
                         ≻ 
                         y 
                         ≻ 
                         z 
                         ≻ 
                         w 
                         
                       stands for  
                         
                            { 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                            , 
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               , 
                               z 
                               ) 
                            
                            , 
                            
                               ( 
                               z 
                               , 
                               w 
                               ) 
                            
                            } 
                         
                         ⊆ 
                         
                         ≻ 
                         
                      .

Notice that the (strict or weak)  
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property is the classical Ferrers condition, whereas the (strict or weak)  
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property is semitransitivity. Weak and strict Ferrers properties behave very differently: they coincide only in the classical cases of interval orders and semiorders, that is, for  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            { 
                            
                               ( 
                               2 
                               , 
                               2 
                               ) 
                            
                            , 
                            
                               ( 
                               3 
                               , 
                               1 
                               ) 
                            
                            } 
                         
                      , otherwise displaying an opposite behavior. For future reference, below we summarize some preliminary facts proved in  Giarlotta and Watson (2014) .

Warning:  To simplify the statement of the next lemma, whenever we say that a  strict  preference  
                         ≻ 
                       is  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, we mean that it is  strictly  
                      
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers; similarly, if we say that a  weak  preference  
                         ≿ 
                       is  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, then we mean that it is  weakly  
                      
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers. Similarly, we omit the use of the attributes “strict” and “weak” for binary relations whenever the meaning is clear from context: for instance, we say “ 
                         ≻ 
                       is a partial order” in place of “ 
                         ≻ 
                       is a strict partial order”, “ 
                         ≿ 
                       is a semiorder” in place of “ 
                         ≿ 
                       is a weak semiorder”, etc.

The one-way implications in parts (ii), (vi) and (vii) cannot be reversed.

Loosely speaking, weak Ferrers properties are connected to degrees of transitivity of the indifference  
                         ∼ 
                       associated to a preference, whereas strict Ferrers properties deal with degrees of transitivity of the strict preference  
                         ≻ 
                      . In fact, weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties already guarantee the transitivity of the strict preference  
                         ≻ 
                       starting from  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      , and all successive properties imply stronger and stronger forms of transitivity of the indifference  
                         ∼ 
                      , until the weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            3 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property gives the full transitivity of  
                         ≿ 
                         
                      . For a full description of all implications among combinations of weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties, see  Giarlotta and Watson (2014) .

On the other hand, strict Ferrers properties are informative of the degree of transitivity of the strict preference  
                         ≻ 
                      . The strongest property of all is the strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, which ensures not only the transitivity of  
                         ≻ 
                      , but also that of the associated indifference  
                         ∼ 
                      . The strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers and  
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers still imply the transitivity of  
                         ≻ 
                      , but the transitivity of  
                         ∼ 
                       is not guaranteed any longer. Starting from the strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            4 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers and  
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, the transitivity of  
                         ≻ 
                       may fail. Furthermore, in case  
                         ≻ 
                       is not transitive, the strength of strict Ferrers properties ceases to be monotonicallydecreasing.

The goal of this paper is a complete analysis of strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties. Thus, for the readers’ convenience (and at the cost of being redundant), we conclude this section by explicitly restating  Definition 2.2  in the special case of strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties.

In this section, we do a brief detour from the technicalities of the paper, with the purpose of motivating the study of strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties from the point of view of economics and psychology. In fact, we relate the satisfaction of Ferrers properties to the avoidance of suitable “money-pump phenomena”  (Cubit & Sugden, 2001; Davidson et al., 1955) . The reader may find it useful to also consult Section 4.3 of  Cantone et al. (2016) , where a similar analysis is done with respect to weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties.

If an economic agent is willing to pay $10 for a good  
                         X 
                      , then it is reasonable to say that the agent weakly prefers the good  
                         X 
                       to $10. On the other hand, if an agent is willing to sell a good  
                         Y 
                       for $10, then it is reasonable to say that the agent weakly prefers the $10 to  
                         Y 
                      . Therefore, if an agent is willing to pay $10 for a good  
                         X 
                      , and is also willing to sell a good  
                         Y 
                       for $10, then it is reasonable to say that the agent weakly prefers the good  
                         X 
                       to $10, and also weakly prefers the $10 to  
                         Y 
                      . However, if weak preference is not transitive, we may not be able to deduce that the agent prefers  
                         X 
                       over  
                         Y 
                      . So this reveals an alternative (weaker) form of weakpreference.

Notice that we have used cash as measure of value, but any  fungible  and  perfectly divisible  good can replace cash. Thus, we can say, for instance, that if an agent is willing to trade quantity  
                         q 
                       of a fungible and perfectly divisible good  
                         G 
                       for a good  
                         X 
                      , and is also willing to sell a good  
                         Y 
                       for quantity  
                         q 
                       of good  
                         G 
                      , then it is reasonable that the same agent weakly prefers the good  
                         X 
                       to quantity  
                         q 
                       of good  
                         G 
                      , and also weakly prefers quantity  
                         q 
                       of good  
                         G 
                       to  
                         Y 
                      . But again, if weak preference is not transitive, then we may not be able to deduce that the agent prefers  
                         X 
                       over  
                         Y 
                      . Again, this reveals a weaker form of weak preference.

In fact, the assumption of perfectly divisibility need not be required, so long as the amount of the good needed to compare  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Y 
                       is available. Neither is fungibility required, so long as the same specimen of the good is compared to both  
                         X 
                       and  
                         Y 
                      . Summarizing, if an agent is willing to sell good  
                         G 
                       for a good  
                         X 
                      , and is also willing to sell good  
                         Y 
                       for good  
                         G 
                      , then it is reasonable to say that the agent weakly prefers  
                         X 
                       to  
                         G 
                      , and also weakly prefers  
                         G 
                       to  
                         Y 
                      , but still may not prefer  
                         X 
                       over  
                         Y 
                      . This situation yields the following notion:

Definition 3.1 
                         Given goods  
                               X 
                             and  
                               Y 
                            , if there is a good  
                               G 
                             such that  
                               X 
                             is weakly preferred to G, and  
                               G 
                             is weakly preferred to  
                               Y 
                            , then we say  
                               X 
                             is  cash-value preferred  to  
                               Y 
                            .

Given goods  
                               X 
                             and  
                               Y 
                            , if there is a good  
                               G 
                             such that  
                               X 
                             is weakly preferred to G, and  
                               G 
                             is weakly preferred to  
                               Y 
                            , then we say  
                               X 
                             is  cash-value preferred  to  
                               Y 
                            .

Essentially cash-value preference is the strengthened weak preference an agent arrives at when required to assign cash-value to goods:  
                         X 
                       is cash-value preferred to  
                         Y 
                       if  
                         X 
                       is weakly preferred to an amount of cash which is weakly preferred to  
                         Y 
                      .

Next, we relate cash-value preferences to chains of strict transitivity.

Definition 3.2 
                         An asymmetric preference  
                               ≻ 
                             is  
                               m 
                            
                            -strictly transitive  
                            
                               6 
                             
                            
                               6 
                               Equivalently, an asymmetric relation is strictly  
                                     m 
                                  -transitive if there is no mixed cycle composed of  
                                     m 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                   strict preferences followed by an indifference/incomparability. This is a special case of a notion of  strict  
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        m 
                                        , 
                                        n 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  -transitivity , which is connected to the avoidance of certain mixed cycles of preferences/indifferences (cf. Section  10 ). 
                            if  
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ≻

                               ≻

                                     m

                                     2

                                     m 
                                     + 
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            .

An asymmetric preference  
                               ≻ 
                             is  
                               m 
                            
                            -strictly transitive  
                            
                               6 
                             
                            
                               6 
                               Equivalently, an asymmetric relation is strictly  
                                     m 
                                  -transitive if there is no mixed cycle composed of  
                                     m 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                   strict preferences followed by an indifference/incomparability. This is a special case of a notion of  strict  
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        m 
                                        , 
                                        n 
                                        )

                            if

                               ≻

Thus, in particular,  
                         1 
                      -strictly transitive is the same as transitive. We can interpret  
                         m 
                      -strictly transitive as saying that if we have a chain of  
                         m 
                         + 
                         1 
                       strict preferences of one good over the next, then the last good cannot be weakly preferred over the first good.

If there were a chain of  
                         m 
                         + 
                         1 
                       strict preferences of one good over the next, and the last good were to be weakly preferred over the first good, then the agent could be exploited as a money pump. For each strict preference, the agent would be willing to pay money to exchange one good for another one. The agent could then be led around the cycle over and over, constantly spending money, and getting nowhere. It is reasonable to hypothesize that such cycles should not exist. However, because each exchange of goods may take time, goods may not be immediately available on the market, and each exchange may exact a transaction cost, such cycles may reasonably exist too.

The next result concludes this digression on a possible semantics of strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties by showing that these axioms can be interpreted as the absence of a certain chain of strict preferences of one good over the next, where the last good is cash-value preferred over the first good. Its proof readily follows from the definitions of strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property (  Definition 2.4 ) and cash-value preference (  Definition 3.1 ).

An asymmetric preference

The implications stated in  Lemma 2.3  (vi)–(vii) describe a monotonic behavior of weak and strict Ferrers properties: increasing along with  
                         m 
                       and  
                         n 
                       in the former case, and decreasing in the latter case. Nevertheless, this inverse behavior is far from being a duality between the two types of properties. For instance, the decreasing strength of strict Ferrers properties only holds under the additional assumption that the strict preference is transitive, whereas, in the absence of this hypothesis, some implications among strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties fail to hold: for instance, for strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties, we have  
                         
                            ( 
                            4 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers  
                         ⇏ 
                      
                      
                         
                            ( 
                            5 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers,  
                         
                            ( 
                            6 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers  
                         ⇏ 
                      
                      
                         
                            ( 
                            9 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers,  
                         
                            ( 
                            7 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers  
                         ⇏ 
                      
                      
                         
                            ( 
                            8 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers,  
                         
                            ( 
                            8 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers  
                         ⇏ 
                      
                      
                         
                            ( 
                            9 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers or  
                         
                            ( 
                            10 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, etc.

The first goal of this paper is to establish a form of regularity for strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties by showing that – upon partitioning them in two classes, according to the parity of  
                         m 
                       – their strength somehow diminishes as  
                         m 
                       increases. In fact, we shall prove that the implication among strict Ferrers properties “eventually stabilizes” in the following sense: for each  
                         m 
                         ≥ 
                         2 
                      , there exists a least integer  
                         
                            st 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                         ≥ 
                         m 
                      , called the  stabilizer  of  
                         m 
                      , with the property that an arbitrary strict preference that is  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers is also  
                         
                            ( 
                            p 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers for each  
                         p 
                         ≥ 
                         
                            st 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Thus the stabilizer  
                         
                            st 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                       of  
                         m 
                       provides a discrete measure of the “limit strength” of the strict  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property: the smaller the value of  
                         
                            st 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                      , the stronger the  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property.

Terminology 4.1 
                         
                            Henceforth, all Ferrers properties under examination are strict. Further, by “preference” we mean strict preference, that is, an asymmetric binary relation.

Henceforth, all Ferrers properties under examination are strict. Further, by “preference” we mean strict preference, that is, an asymmetric binary relation.

To start, we endow the set of integers greater than or equal to  
                         2 
                       with a partial order, which is contained in the reversed linear order of the natural numbers. To enhance readability, we shall use  
                         m 
                       and  
                         n 
                       as integers, usually taking  
                         m 
                       greater than  
                         n 
                      .

Recall that a  
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers irreflexive preference is a  partial semiorder  (see  Fishburn (1985) , p. 18), and so this property has already been studied. Further, partial semiorders are very close to semiorders (see  Fishburn (1985) , Theorem 1, p. 21). 
                         7 
                       
                      
                         7 
                         We thank one of the referees for pointing out this fact. 
                      Our analysis of the strict Ferrers ordering aims at getting further insight into the topic, by considering cases in which there are sequences of strict preferences for which transitivity may not hold.

Before starting our study of the strict Ferrers ordering, we introduce another piece of terminology, which will be used throughout the paper.

The next two lemmas determine the relationship between  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties on one hand, and strict transitivity and acyclicity on the other one. 
                         8 
                       
                      
                         8 
                         Here by “strict transitivity” we mean the transitivity of a strict preference, which is equivalent to the quasi-transitivity of the associated weak preference. 
                      Concerning transitivity, which is the usual framework for studying semiorders and interval orders, we have:

Even if transitivity may fail (according to  Lemma 4.7  (ii)), an  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers asymmetric preference always satisfies the weakest form of transitivity, namely, acyclicity:

Lemma 4.8 
                         
                            Any  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            -Ferrers property implies acyclicity. The converse does not hold.

Any  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            -Ferrers property implies acyclicity. The converse does not hold.

Proof 
                         By  Lemma 2.3 , the result holds for  
                               m 
                               = 
                               2 
                               , 
                               3 
                            . Thus let  
                               ≻ 
                             be an asymmetric relation on a set  
                               X 
                             satisfying the  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                            -Ferrers property for some  
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               4 
                            . Toward a contradiction, let  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ≻ 
                               … 
                               ≻ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     k 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             be a cycle of minimal length. Since  
                               ≻ 
                             is asymmetric (hence irreflexive), it follows that  
                               k 
                               ≥ 
                               4 
                            . Consider the path obtained by gluing together several copies of the cycle until its length is greater than  
                               m 
                               + 
                               1 
                            , namely,  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x

                                     ≻

                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≻ 
                                     … 
                                     ≻

                                           k 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≻ 
                                     … 
                                     ≻

                                           k 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≻

                                     ≻ 
                                     … 
                                     ≻

                                           k

                            , using irreflexivity and asymmetry.) It follows that there exists  
                               i

                         The converse implication does not hold, as the following example shows. Let

                                     k 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            , contradicting the minimality of the original cycle.

The converse implication does not hold, as the following example shows. Let  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  N 
                                  , 
                                  < 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be the strict linear ordering on the natural numbers, and  
                               1 
                             the strict chain with a unique element. Let  
                               N 
                               ⊕ 
                               1 
                             be the  free union  of  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  N 
                                  , 
                                  < 
                                  ) 
                               
                             and  
                               1 
                            , that is, the asymmetric ordering on  
                               N 
                               ∪ 
                               1 
                             obtained by taking the disjoint union of the two linear orderings. Then  
                               N 
                               ⊕ 
                               1 
                             is acyclic, but it satisfies no  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                            -Ferrers property.  □

The next result says that any “long” path in an  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers preference does have plenty of edges from its first element toward its tail.

Theorem 4.10  is refined in the next two sections, where we determine all Ferrers stabilizers.

The computation of the stabilizer poses a different degree of difficulty for even and odd integers: simple for the former, rather articulated for the latter. We compute the stabilizer of even integers in this section, and delay that of odd integers to the next section. To start, we introduce the notion of a spanning order on a finite set.

Definition 5.2 
                         A  (strict) spanning order  on  
                               X 
                               =

                               ≻

                               1 
                            . (Thus, in particular,  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1

                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               ≻ 
                               … 
                               ≻ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                             holds.) 
                               9 
                             
                            
                               9 
                               Notice that here acyclicity is equivalent to require that  
                                     ¬ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              j

                                     i 
                                     , 
                                     j 
                                   such that  
                                     1 
                                     ≤ 
                                     i 
                                     < 
                                     j 
                                     ≤ 
                                     n 
                                  .

A  (strict) spanning order  on  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        1

                               ≻

                               1

                                     i 
                                     , 
                                     j

                                     1 
                                     ≤ 
                                     i 
                                     < 
                                     j 
                                     ≤ 
                                     n 
                                  .

In other words, a spanning order is an asymmetric relation obtained by enriching the “base chain”, which connects all elements from the first to the last, with some forward edges (whereas backward edges are not allowed). Next, using the notion of a spanning order, we prove a lemma that serves two purposes. First, it is the key fact in the process of determining the stabilizer of even numbers. Second, it starts pointing out that even and odd integers behave rather differently.

Lemma 5.3 
                         
                            For each integer  
                            
                               n 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                            
                            , we have: 
                         
                         
                            
                               
                                  (i) 
                                  
                                     
                                        n 
                                        ↷̸ 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           2 
                                           n 
                                           − 
                                           3 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                      
                                     if  
                                     
                                        n

Claim:

We already know that a necessary condition for having

                         ↷ 
                         m

                         n

Due to its technicality, the proof of  Theorem 6.1  is delayed to later sections. Specifically, its sufficiency is proved in Section  7 , whereas its (more challenging) necessity is proved in Section  9 , using some preliminary results obtained in Section  8 . As we shall see, the main difficulty in the proof of  Theorem 6.1  is to find suitable counterexamples showing that if  
                         m 
                         < 
                         2 
                         n 
                         − 
                         3 
                       and  
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            n 
                            − 
                            3 
                            − 
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                       does not divide  
                         
                            ( 
                            n 
                            − 
                            3 
                            ) 
                         
                      , then  
                         n 
                       does not arrow  
                         m 
                      .

The characterization given in  Theorem 6.1  is simple but not easy to use. An easier way to compute all  
                         n 
                         , 
                         
                         m 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            
                               N 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                       such that  
                         n 
                       arrows  
                         m 
                       is by working along the diagonals, i.e., stating the implication for each  
                         k 
                         ≥ 
                         1 
                       when  
                         n 
                         ↷ 
                         n 
                         + 
                         k 
                      . The following immediate consequence of  Theorem 6.1  does the job:

Corollary 6.2 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               k 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            be such that  
                            
                               1 
                               ≤ 
                               k 
                               < 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                            
                            . Then  
                            
                               n 
                               ↷ 
                               n 
                               + 
                               k 
                             
                            if and only if  
                            
                               n 
                             
                            equals  
                            
                               k 
                               + 
                               3 
                             
                            plus a divisor of  
                            
                               k 
                            
                            .

Let  
                            
                               k 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            be such that  
                            
                               1 
                               ≤ 
                               k 
                               < 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                            
                            . Then  
                            
                               n 
                               ↷ 
                               n 
                               + 
                               k

                               n 
                             
                            equals  
                            
                               k
