                               3 
                             
                            plus a divisor of  
                            
                               k 
                            
                            .

Corollary 6.2  enables us to easily compute all relationships  
                         n 
                         ↷ 
                         m 
                       in the strict Ferrers ordering: these implications are displayed in  Table 1 , where we put  
                         n 
                       on the  
                         y 
                      -axis, and  
                         m 
                         = 
                         n 
                         + 
                         k 
                         ≥ 
                         n 
                       on the  
                         x 
                      -axis. We put a checkmark “✓” when the implication  
                         n 
                         ↷ 
                         m 
                         = 
                         n 
                         + 
                         k 
                       holds; we do this by diagonals, i.e., fixed  
                         k 
                      . Specifically, when  
                         k 
                         ≥ 
                         n 
                         − 
                         2 
                      , put all ✓; further, when  
                         k 
                         = 
                         n 
                         − 
                         3 
                      , put ✓ for odd  
                         n 
                      ; finally, when  
                         k 
                         ≤ 
                         n 
                         − 
                         4 
                      , put ✓ for only finitely many  
                         n 
                      , namely those  
                         n 
                       that are equal to  
                         k 
                         + 
                         3 
                       plus a divisor of  
                         k 
                      .

Corollary 4.6  says that the strict Ferrers ordering  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               ↷ 
                            
                         
                       is a poset having  
                         2 
                       as maximum.  Theorem 6.1  implies that this result cannot be strengthened by showing that it is a lattice (contrary to what happens for the finite lattice of weak  
                         
                            ( 
                            m 
                            , 
                            n 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers properties). 
                         10 
                       
                      
                         10 
                         This contrasts what is written in the second statement of Conjecture 2.16 in  Giarlotta and Watson (2014) , where “lattice with a zero” should be substituted by “poset with a maximum”.

Corollary 6.3 
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           N 
                                        
                                        
                                           ∗ 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ↷ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            is neither a meet-semilattice nor a join-semilattice.

N 
                                        
                                        
                                           ∗ 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ↷ 
                                  
                               
                             
                            is neither a meet-semilattice nor a join-semilattice.

Proof 
                         
                            Corollary 4.6  yields that  
                               31 
                             only arrows the integers in the set  
                               
                                  { 
                                  31 
                                  , 
                                  45 
                                  , 
                                  52 
                                  , 
                                  55 
                                  } 
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  57 
                                  , 
                                  ∞ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Similarly,  
                               33 
                             only arrows the integers in the set  
                               
                                  { 
                                  33 
                                  , 
                                  48 
                                  , 
                                  53 
                                  , 
                                  57 
                                  , 
                                  58 
                                  } 
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  60 
                                  , 
                                  ∞ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . In particular, we have  
                               31 
                               ↷ 
                               57 
                               , 
                               58 
                             and  
                               33 
                               ↷ 
                               57 
                               , 
                               58 
                            . Since  
                               57 
                             and  
                               58 
                             are incomparable in the strict Ferrers ordering, and  
                               31 
                             and  
                               33 
                             have no other common predecessor, it follows that  
                               31 
                             and  
                               33 
                             have no meet (greatest lower bound). For the same reason,  
                               57 
                             and  
                               58 
                             have no join (least upper bound). Thus  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           N 
                                        
                                        
                                           ∗ 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ↷ 
                                  
                               
                             is neither a meet-semilattice nor a join-semilattice.  □

Corollary 4.6  yields that  
                               31 
                             only arrows the integers in the set  
                               
                                  { 
                                  31 
                                  , 
                                  45 
                                  , 
                                  52 
                                  , 
                                  55 
                                  } 
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  57 
                                  , 
                                  ∞ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Similarly,  
                               33 
                             only arrows the integers in the set  
                               
                                  { 
                                  33 
                                  , 
                                  48 
                                  , 
                                  53 
                                  , 
                                  57 
                                  , 
                                  58 
                                  } 
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  60 
                                  , 
                                  ∞ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . In particular, we have  
                               31 
                               ↷ 
                               57 
                               , 
                               58 
                             and  
                               33 
                               ↷ 
                               57 
                               , 
                               58 
                            . Since  
                               57 
                             and  
                               58 
                             are incomparable in the strict Ferrers ordering, and  
                               31 
                             and  
                               33 
                             have no other common predecessor, it follows that  
                               31 
                             and  
                               33 
                             have no meet (greatest lower bound). For the same reason,  
                               57 
                             and  
                               58 
                             have no join (least upper bound). Thus  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           N 
                                        
                                        
                                           ∗ 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ↷ 
                                  
                               
                             is neither a meet-semilattice nor a join-semilattice.  □

The most important consequence of  Theorem 6.1  extends the results of  Corollary 5.4  to odd numbers, allowing us to determine a simple formula for the computation of the Ferrers stabilizer of all integers. The following notion is needed in the computation:

The following (surprisingly simple) formula delivers the stabilizer of all integers:

Corollary 6.6 
                         
                            
                               
                                  st 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                               − 
                               
                                  run 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            for each  
                            
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                            
                            .

st 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                               − 
                               
                                  run 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            for each  
                            
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     N 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                            
                            .

                         Let  
                               n 
                               ∈

                                     ∗ 
                                  
                               
                            . If  
                               n 
                             is even, then

                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               − 
                               1 
                             by definition, and  
                               
                                  st 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n

                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               2

                                  ( 
                                  n

                               = 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                               − 
                               
                                  run 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                             holds for all even  
                               n 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                            . 
                         Next, suppose  
                               n 
                             is odd. If  
                               n 
                               = 
                               3 
                            , then  
                               
                                  run

                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             by definition, and

                                  3

                               = 
                               3 
                             by  Lemma 4.4 . Thus the claim holds for  
                               n 
                               = 
                               3 
                            . Now suppose  
                               n

                               2

                               n

                               m

                               m

                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3

                               n 
                               ↷ 
                               m

                               m 
                             such that  
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                               − 
                               
                                  run 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ≤ 
                               m 
                               ≤ 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               4 
                             and  
                               n 
                               ↷̸ 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               4 
                               − 
                               
                                  run 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . By definition of running index, all integers  
                               p 
                               ∈

                                  1 
                                  ,

                                  ,

                                  }

                               n

                               3 
                            , whereas  
                               
                                  run 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                               + 
                               1 
                             is not.  Theorem 6.1  (ii.1) implies that

                                        n 
                                        ↷ 
                                        m 
                                      for all  
                                        m 
                                        ≤ 
                                        2 
                                        n 
                                        − 
                                        4

                                        n 
                                        − 
                                        m 
                                        −

                                        ∈ 
                                        
                                           {

                                     .

In  Table 2  we describe the strict Ferrers ordering for some odd integers, according to the findings of  Theorem 6.1  and Corollary 6.6 .

In this section, we show that (ii) implies (i) in the statement of  Theorem 6.1 .

Let  
                         2 
                         ≤ 
                         n 
                         < 
                         m 
                      . If (ii.3) holds, then  
                         n 
                       arrows  
                         m 
                       by  Theorem 4.10 . Further, if (ii.2) holds, then  
                         n 
                       arrows  
                         m 
                       by  Lemma 5.3  (ii). Thus, it suffices to show that if (ii.1) holds, then  
                         n 
                       arrows  
                         m 
                      . To that end, the key result is the following “reduction lemma”, whose proof is similar to that of  Lemma 5.3  (ii):

We now prove the main result of this section.

Proof of Lemma 7.1 
                         Let  
                               m 
                               , 
                               n 
                               , 
                               k 
                             be positive integers such that  
                               m 
                               = 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                               − 
                               k 
                               > 
                               n 
                             and  
                               k 
                             divides  
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                            . First, notice that  
                               n 
                               > 
                               3 
                            . Next, we claim that  
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               n 
                               + 
                               k 
                            . To prove that, observe that if  
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                               < 
                               2 
                               k 
                             were to hold, then the hypothesis  
                               k 
                               
                               | 
                               
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                             would imply  
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                               = 
                               k 
                            , hence  
                               m 
                               = 
                               2 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  + 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                               − 
                               3 
                               − 
                               k 
                               = 
                               k 
                               + 
                               3 
                               = 
                               n 
                            , which is false. Therefore, we have  
                               n 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                               k 
                               + 
                               3 
                            , and so  
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               n 
                               + 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  k 
                                  + 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                               − 
                               3 
                               − 
                               k 
                               = 
                               n 
                               + 
                               k 
                            , as claimed. 
                         Now suppose  
                               ≻ 
                             is an  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  , 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                            -Ferrers preference on  
                               X 
                            , and let  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                             be such that  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ≻ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               ≻ 
                               … 
                               ≻ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                               
                            . If  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ≻ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     − 
                                     k 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             holds, then the chain  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ≻ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     − 
                                     k 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ≻ 
                               … 
                               ≻ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                               
                             has length  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  3 
                                  − 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                               − 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  k 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                               + 
                               1 
                               = 
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            , and so this is the required chain of

In this section, we do two things: (1) we construct some “homogeneous” preferences, characterized by the fact that, for any possible length, they have either all edges having that length or none of them; (2) we introduce a condition of “internal”  
                         
                            ( 
                            n 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers, which is in general weaker than the  
                         
                            ( 
                            n 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers property but suffices to prove the latter in suitable cases. Then, homogeneous preferences will provide us with the counterexamples needed to complete the proof of  Theorem 6.1 , whereas the internal  
                         
                            ( 
                            n 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers condition will play a main role in verifying that a homogeneous preference is  
                         
                            ( 
                            n 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                      -Ferrers.

Definition 8.1 
                         Fix an integer  
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                            . Given a (typically infinite) set  
                               E 
                             of nonnegative integers, let  
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  E 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  m 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be the  homogeneous  preference on the set  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                  
                                  } 
                               
                             defined by 
                               11 
                             
                            
                               11 
                               In what follows, we shall always use the notation  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ↣ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   in place of  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≻ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  , with the purpose of emphasizing the fact that we are dealing with homogeneous preferences. Further,  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ↣̸ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   stands for  
                                     ¬ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                        
                                        ↣ 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              j 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  . 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ↣ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           ⟺ 
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              def 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     
                                     j 
                                     − 
                                     i 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                     ∈ 
                                     E 
                                  
                               
                            for all  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            . The set  
                               E 
                             is the  homogeneity  of  
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  E 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  m 
                                  ) 
                               
                            .

Fix an integer  
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                            . Given a (typically infinite) set  
                               E 
                             of nonnegative integers, let  
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  E 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  m 
                                  ) 
                               
                             be the  homogeneous  preference on the set  
                               X 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                  
                                  } 
                               
                             defined by 
                               11 
                             
                            
                               11 
                               In what follows, we shall always use the notation  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ↣ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   in place of  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≻ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                  , with the purpose of emphasizing the fact that we are dealing with homogeneous preferences. Further,  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ↣̸ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                   stands for  
                                     ¬ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              i 
                                           
                                        
                                        ↣ 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              j 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  . 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ↣ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           j 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           ⟺ 
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              def 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     
                                     j 
                                     − 
                                     i 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                     ∈ 
                                     E 
                                  
                               
                            for all  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            . The set  
                               E 
                             is the  homogeneity  of  
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  E 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  m 
                                  ) 
                               
                            .

Observe that the set of homogeneous lengths of edges in  
                         P 
                         
                            ( 
                            E 
                            , 
                            
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                       is obtained by adding  
                         1 
                       to all the integers in  
                         E 
                      . 
                         12 
                       
                      
                         12 
                         At this point, one may wonder why we do not simply define “ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ↣ 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                             if and only if  
                               j 
                               − 
                               i 
                               ∈ 
                               E 
                            ”, thus making  
                               E 
                             into the set of all allowed lengths of homogeneous edges. The reason for our choice is technical: in fact, we will later prove some closure properties of the set  
                               E 
                            , which require  
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  E 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  m 
                                  ) 
                               
                             to be as in  Definition 8.1 . 
                      Further, notice that since  
                         E 
                         ⊆ 
                         N 
                      , we only have “forward arrows” in  
                         P 
                         
                            ( 
                            E 
                            , 
                            
                            m 
                            ) 
                         
                      , that is,  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         ↣ 
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                       implies  
                         i 
                         < 
                         j 
                      .

Notation 8.2 
                         Henceforth  
                               E 
                             is always a set of nonnegative integers, even without explicit mention. Further, any interval is always to be intended in  
                               N 
                            : for instance, by  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  10 
                                  , 
                                  ∞ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             we mean the set of integers  
                               
                                  { 
                                  p 
                                  ∈ 
                                  N 
                                  : 
                                  p 
                                  ≥ 
                                  10 
                                  } 
                               
                            .

Henceforth  
                               E 
                             is always a set of nonnegative integers, even without explicit mention. Further, any interval is always to be intended in  
                               N 
                            : for instance, by  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  10 
                                  , 
                                  ∞

                             we mean the set of integers  
                               
                                  { 
                                  p

                                  :

                                  ≥ 
                                  10 
                                  } 
                               
                            .

This is the generalization of  Example 8.4 , and it has been already examined in a disguised form: see the proof of  Lemma 5.3  (i).  □

We now switch to the analysis of the internal Ferrers condition, and then finally connect it to homogeneous preferences. To start, we introduce two preliminary notions, which establish a relation between two distinct positive integers  
                         m 
                       and  
                         n 
                       with respect to a given subset  
                         E 
                       of the natural numbers: (i) coverability, and(ii) alterability. The notion of coverability may appear quite obscure at a first sight; however, its rationale will become clear later on.

Definition 8.6 
                         Let  
                               m 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈

                               ≤ 
                               n 
                               < 
                               m

                               E 
                               ⊆ 
                               N 
                            . We say that  
                               E 
                             is

                                  n

As the proof of  Example 8.7  suggests, any subset of  
                         N 
                       is coverable by pairs of integers that are “not too far apart”.

Lemma 8.8 
                         
                            For any  
                            
                               m 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               N 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               E 
                               ⊆ 
                               N 
                            
                            , if  
                            
                               5 
                               < 
                               n 
                               < 
                               m 
                               ≤ 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               5 
                            
                            , then  
                            
                               E 
                             
                            is  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            -coverable.

For any  
                            
                               m 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈

                               ⊆

                            is  
                            
                               
                                  (

                                  ,

                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            -coverable.

Proof 
                         Let  
                               m 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               N 
                             be such that  
                               5 
                               < 
                               n 
                               < 
                               m 
                               ≤ 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               5 
                            , and  
                               E 
                               ⊆ 
                               N 
                             arbitrary. Suppose  
                               e 
                             is a sum of numbers in  
                               E 
                            , possibly with repetitions, and  
                               0 
                               < 
                               k 
                               < 
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                               + 
                               e 
                               < 
                               m 
                            . If we take  
                               
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                             and let  
                               
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                             run over  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  2 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                                  ] 
                               
                            , then  
                               
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               − 
                               1 
                             can equal any  
                               k 
                             in  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  2 
                                  ] 
                               
                            . If we take  
                               
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               e 
                             and let  
                               
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                             run over  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  2 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                                  ] 
                               
                            , then  
                               
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               − 
                               1 
                             can equal any  
                               k 
                             in  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  e 
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  e 
                                  + 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  2 
                                  ] 
                               
                            . Together  
                               
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               − 
                               1 
                             can reach any value in  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  2 
                                  ] 
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  e 
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  e 
                                  + 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  2 
                                  ] 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  e 
                                  + 
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  2 
                                  ] 
                               
                            , where the last equality follows from  
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                               + 
                               e 
                               < 
                               m 
                               ≤ 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               5 
                            , and so  
                               e 
                               + 
                               1 
                               < 
                               n 
                               − 
                               3 
                            .  □

Let  
                               m 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               N 
                             be such that  
                               5 
                               < 
                               n 
                               < 
                               m 
                               ≤ 
                               2 
                               n 
                               − 
                               5 
                            , and  
                               E

The next result clarifies the relationship between the notions of coverability and alterability.

Lemma 8.11 
                         
                            Assume that  
                            
                               E 
                               ⊆ 
                               N 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               m 
                               , 
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               N 
                             
                            satisfy the following properties: 
                         
                         
                            
                               
                                  (i) 
                                  
                                     
                                        5 
                                        < 
                                        n 
                                        < 
                                        m 
                                        ≤ 
                                        2 
                                        n 
                                        − 
                                        5 
                                     
                                     ;

                                        E

                                        n 
                                        − 
                                        4 
                                     
                                     ;

                                        0 
                                        ∈ 
                                        E 
                                     
                                     .

                               E 
                             
                            is  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            -coverable, then it is  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            -alterable.

Assume that  
                            
                               E 
                               ⊆ 
                               N

                                        n 
                                        −

                                        − 
                                        4

                                  (iii) 
                                  
                                     
                                        0 
                                        ∈ 
                                        E 
                                     
                                     .

5 
                                        < 
                                        n 
                                        <

                                        n

                                     is closed under finite sums with total strictly less than

                                        ∈ 
                                        E 
                                     
                                     .

                               E

                                  m

                            -coverable, then it is

                         Assume that  
                               E 
                             is

                                  )

                               σ

                               t

                               < 
                               ⋯

                               <

                               σ

                                  ,

                               −

                               −

                             is a integer in

                            . Set

                                  − 
                                  1 
                                  )

Intuitively, “internal” stands for the fact that the only configurations to be tested are those such that the element on the right end side is already present among the elements on the left end side (and is different from the first and the last). Here we complete the proof of  Theorem 6.1 . To that end, we prove several technical lemmas, which will readily yield the main fact ( Corollary 9.12 ). For readers’ convenience, the statement of each lemma is preceded by an example, which deals with a special case.
